perturbation.original_text: Owner covenants that it will maintain at all times during the Term of this Agreement insurance coverage for sudden and accidental pollution of $500,000,000.
perturbation.changed_text: Owner will maintain insurance coverage for pollution during the Term of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific amount ($500,000,000) for sudden and accidental pollution insurance creates a potential legal issue. The contract becomes vague, potentially violating federal regulations that require specific levels of financial responsibility for oil spills.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Oil Pollution Act of 1990 (OPA 90) and related regulations under 33 U.S. Code § 2704
perturbation.law_explanation: OPA 90 mandates specific financial responsibility (insurance) requirements for vessels transporting oil to cover potential spill cleanup costs and damages. Removing the specified coverage amount contradicts the intent and practical application of these requirements, as it allows for potentially insufficient coverage, thus violating the law's underlying purpose.

Limits on liability (a) General rule Except as otherwise provided in this section, the total of the liability of a responsible party under section 2702 of this title and any removal costs incurred by, or on behalf of, the responsible party, with respect to each incident shall not exceed- (1) for a tank vessel the greater of- (A) with respect to a single-hull vessel, including a single-hull v
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the specific insurance coverage amount of $500,000,000 for sudden and accidental pollution, which creates a contradiction. The Law Explanation states that OPA 90 mandates specific financial responsibility (insurance) requirements. The original text ensures a specific level of coverage, aligning with the intent of OPA 90. By omitting the specified coverage amount, the changed text introduces the possibility of insufficient coverage, undermining the financial responsibility requirements for pollution incidents. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Limits on liability' under 33 USC 2704, which implies that there are requirements regarding liability and, by extension, financial coverage.

perturbation.original_text: Owner warrants that it will maintain and enforce at all times during the Term of this Agreement a drug and alcohol abuse policy applicable to the vessels which complies in all material respects with the minimum standards promulgated by the U.S. Coast Guard.
perturbation.changed_text: Owner will maintain a drug and alcohol abuse policy applicable to the vessels during the Term of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Omitting the requirement that the drug and alcohol policy comply 'in all material respects with the minimum standards promulgated by the U.S. Coast Guard' weakens the clause. This could violate federal regulations requiring specific drug and alcohol testing and prevention programs for maritime personnel.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 46 CFR § 16.201 - Chemical testing following serious marine incidents.
perturbation.law_explanation: 46 CFR § 16.201 mandates specific procedures and standards for drug and alcohol testing following serious marine incidents. By removing the requirement that the Owner's policy complies with USCG standards, the contract potentially allows for a policy that does not meet these mandatory testing requirements, leading to non-compliance and legal liability in the event of an incident.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 46 CFR § 16.201 - Application. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 46 CFR § 16.201 - Application. CFR prev | next § 16.201 Application. (a) Chemical testing of personnel must be conducted as required by this subpart and in accordance with the procedures detailed in 49 CFR part 40 . (b) If an individual fails a chemical test for dangerous drugs under this part, the individual will be presumed to be a user of dangerous drugs. (c) If an individual holding a credential fails a chemical test for dangerous drugs , the individual's employer , prospective employer , or sponsoring organization must report the test results in writing to the nearest Coast Guard Officer in Charge, Marine Inspection (OCMI). The individual must be denied employment as a crewmember or must be removed from duties which directly affect the safe operation of the vessel as soon as practicable and is subject to suspension and revocation proceedings against their credential under part 5 of this chapter. (d) If an individual who does not hold a credential fails a chemical test for dangerous drugs , the individual will be denied employment as a crewmember or removed from duties that directly affect the safe operation of the vessel , as soon as possible. (e) An individual who has failed a required chemical test for dangerous drugs may not be re-employed aboard a vessel until the requirements of paragraph (f) of this section and part 5 of this chapter, if applicable, have been satisfied. (f) Before an individual who has failed a required chemical test for dangerous drugs may return to work aboard a vessel , the MRO must determine that the individual is drug-free and the risk of subsequent use of dangerous drugs by that person is sufficiently low to justify their return to work. In addition, the individual must agree to be subject to increased unannounced testing— (1) For a minimum of six (6) tests in the first year af
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates compliance with U.S. Coast Guard minimum standards for drug and alcohol abuse policies. The changed text removes this compliance requirement, potentially allowing for a policy that does not meet those standards. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 46 CFR § 16.201, specifies chemical testing procedures and consequences for failing a drug test. Without the requirement to adhere to USCG standards, the modified contract could permit policies that bypass these mandatory procedures, creating a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation support this interpretation by highlighting the risk of non-compliance with federal regulations concerning drug and alcohol testing.

perturbation.original_text: Owner shall, before and at commencement of each voyage by any vessel under this Agreement, exercise commercially reasonable efforts to ensure that such vessel is seaworthy and in good operating condition, properly manned, equipped and supplied for the voyage, to ensure that the pipes, pumps and coils tight, staunch, are in good operating condition and fit for the voyage, and to ensure that the tanks and other spaces in which product is to be carried are in good operating condition and fit for the carriage and preservation of the same. To the extent required by applicable law, Owner will maintain at all times during the Term of this Agreement a valid and subsisting certificate or other permit issued by the U.S. Coast Guard (or other governmental bureau or department having jurisdiction) approving the applicable vessel for the transportation and carriage of inflammable liquids.
perturbation.changed_text: Owner shall exercise efforts to ensure that such vessel is seaworthy and in operating condition, manned, equipped and supplied for the voyage. To the extent required by applicable law, Owner will maintain at all times during the Term of this Agreement a valid and subsisting certificate or other permit issued by the U.S. Coast Guard (or other governmental bureau or department having jurisdiction) approving the applicable vessel for the transportation and carriage of inflammable liquids.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'commercially reasonable efforts' to 'efforts' significantly lowers the standard of care required of the Owner in ensuring the vessel's seaworthiness. This weakens the obligation and potentially violates the Coast Guard's regulations related to vessel maintenance and safety standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: 46 U.S. Code § 3302 grants the Coast Guard the authority to inspect vessels and ensure compliance with safety regulations. While it doesn't explicitly define 'commercially reasonable efforts,' it implicitly requires a high standard of care for vessel maintenance. Reducing this standard to just 'efforts' creates a legal contradiction because it implies a weaker commitment to maintaining the vessel in compliance with the safety standards that the Coast Guard inspections are meant to enforce. A court could interpret this lower standard as insufficient to meet the implicit requirements of maintaining a seaworthy vessel under USCG regulations.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'commercially reasonable efforts' to 'efforts' weakens the obligation on the Owner to ensure the vessel's seaworthiness. While 46 U.S. Code § 3302, provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, grants the Coast Guard the authority to inspect vessels and ensure compliance with safety regulations, it does not explicitly define 'commercially reasonable efforts,' or the required standard of care. The original text establishes a higher standard for vessel maintenance, while the modified text implies a weaker commitment, creating a potential legal loophole. The reduced standard of care might not align with implicit requirements of maintaining a seaworthy vessel under USCG regulations.
